excerpt: More extreme weather events and record temperatures around the world underline the urgent need for action on climate change, and we are committed to reducing emissions throughout our value chain. Specifically, we have identified five material sources of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions in our value chain with negative impacts on climate change [...] KEY POLICIES: Environmental Policy; Supplier & Licensee Code of Conduct. TARGETS By 2030: Zero carbon emissions at our breweries; All electricity comes from new, renewable assets; 30% reduction in relative value chain carbon emissions. Carbon pricing on our own operations and purchased goods: Our operations result in GHG emissions throughout the value chain, with potentially broad-based impacts contributing to climate change globally. The potential for carbon pricing to increase the costs of purchased goods and the costs of our own operations presents a financial risk to the business. By 2040: Net zero value chain. [...] Overall framework: The Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) provided the framework for our identification, assessment and scenario application of climate-related financial risks and opportunities. This approach was complemented by our existing analyses and research, and the shortlisted risks and opportunities were then discussed with internal stakeholders to deepen understanding and validate the results. [...] Identifying and assessing transition risks and opportunities: To identify transition risks for our operations and broader value chain, we use our GHG inventory, conduct interviews and do desktop research. The GHG inventory helps us estimate the financial impact of carbon pricing and allows us to identify potential locked-in emissions from assets that could slow our transition to a climate-neutral economy, particularly sources beyond electricity consumption. [...] Climate transition plan E1-1 [...] To ensure that our climate action ambitions become a reality, we have anchored our transition plan in our overall business strategy and financial planning processes. Doing so allows us to take into account our organic growth trajectory, with detailed analysis and modelling of climate impacts in the regions where we aim to grow our portfolio most significantly. Creating the transition plan was a collaborative effort, led by ESG target sponsors, our Group Sustainability & ESG function and our Integrated Supply Chain Sustainability function. It was approved according to the Carlsberg governance model, as described in GOV-1. While we still have far to go to reach our targets, we are at a mature stage of implementation, with well-defined ownership and oversight of targets and initiatives, and robust data on our Scope 1, 2 and 3 emissions. [...] We have clear commitments to address climate change, with targets of zero carbon emissions at our breweries by 2030, 100% of our electricity coming from additional renewable capacity by 2030, a 30% reduction in our value chain emissions by 2030 and achieving net zero carbon emissions across our entire value chain by 2040. These targets build on our Environmental Policy commitment to continuously work to reduce emissions across our value chain. Rooted in our ESG programme, our targets have been set to manage material climate-related impacts and risks regarding emissions from our operations and value chain [...] Meeting our targets will require both business transformation and significant investment in decarbonising our physical infrastructure and equipment. [...] updated SBTi requirements and other factors. We will therefore launch updated carbon commitments in Q1 2026, alongside a new climate transition plan.

source_location.subsection: Climate change (E1) / Climate transition plan (E1-1) / Targets and actions (E1-3, E1-4)

CG: ownership and oversight of targets and initiatives
ER: Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures
GHG: zero carbon emissions at our breweries
30% reduction in relative value chain carbon emissions
30% reduction in our value chain emissions by 2030
net zero carbon emissions across our entire value chain by 2040
MBV: overall business strategy and financial planning processes
significant investment in decarbonising our physical infrastructure and equipment

edges_claimed.evidence: Creating the transition plan was a collaborative effort, led by ESG target sponsors, our Group Sustainability & ESG function and our Integrated Supply Chain Sustainability function. It was approved according to the Carlsberg governance model, as described in GOV-1. [...] The Task Force on Climate-related Financial Disclosures (TCFD) provided the framework for our identification, assessment and scenario application of climate-related financial risks and opportunities. [Governance model + ESG target sponsors + Group Sustainability & ESG function drive TCFD-framed disclosure and SBTi target revisions in the ESRS E1 transition plan.]

Rooted in our ESG programme, our targets have been set to manage material climate-related impacts and risks regarding emissions from our operations and value chain, guiding how we are reducing our own carbon footprint [...] updated SBTi requirements and other factors. We will therefore launch updated carbon commitments in Q1 2026, alongside a new climate transition plan. [...] targets of zero carbon emissions at our breweries by 2030 [...] 30% reduction in our value chain emissions by 2030 and achieving net zero carbon emissions across our entire value chain by 2040. [TCFD/SBTi-aligned disclosure + ESRS E1-3/E1-4 targets-and-actions regime drive the net-zero-by-2040 emission-reduction pathway.]

Our operations result in GHG emissions throughout the value chain, with potentially broad-based impacts contributing to climate change globally. The potential for carbon pricing to increase the costs of purchased goods and the costs of our own operations presents a financial risk to the business. [...] To ensure that our climate action ambitions become a reality, we have anchored our transition plan in our overall business strategy and financial planning processes. Doing so allows us to take into account our organic growth trajectory [...] Meeting our targets will require both business transformation and significant investment in decarbonising our physical infrastructure and equipment. [Unmitigated GHG emissions expose the business to carbon-pricing financial risk; the transition plan — anchored in business strategy and financial planning — converts GHG mitigation into organic growth, business transformation and value-preserving investment in decarbonisation, so GHG mitigation raises firm value.]

notes: Relaxed-wording full Chain A preserved across pp.57-59 (ESRS2, Climate change E1) in Carlsberg's climate disclosures — the IRO table (SBM-3 / IRO-1), the climate-related financial risk and opportunity assessment, and the Climate Transition Plan (E1-1 / E1-3 / E1-4). The chain reads: the Carlsberg governance model (GOV-1) + ESG target sponsors + Group Sustainability & ESG function + Integrated Supply Chain Sustainability function (CG, L128-L129) approve and own the transition plan that is framed by TCFD / SBTi / ESRS E1 (ER, L123, L127, L129); TCFD/SBTi-aligned disclosure drives the Scope 1-3 emission-reduction targets — zero brewery emissions and 30% value-chain reduction by 2030 and net zero across the entire value chain by 2040 (GHG, L123-L125, L129); and the transition plan is explicitly anchored in overall business strategy and financial planning, requires business transformation and significant investment in decarbonising physical infrastructure, and shields the business from the carbon-pricing financial risk that unmitigated GHG exposure creates (MBV, L125, L129-L130). Wording tolerances relaxed vs. strict Chain A spec: MBV is anchored on 'financial risk to the business / overall business strategy and financial planning processes / organic growth trajectory / business transformation / significant investment' rather than explicit 'shareholder value / market capitalisation'; ER is anchored on TCFD + SBTi + ESRS E1 / GOV-1 / IRO-1 rather than only CDP/TCFD; CG is anchored on the Carlsberg governance model + ESG target sponsors + the ESG and Supply Chain Sustainability functions. Edge signs match Chain A hypothesis (+, -, -). Causal markers are dense across the passage.
